stratum: Algebra I: fields & univariate polynomials (the engine's substrate)
prerequisites: polynomials over a finite field (degree, roots, evaluation)
the bounded fundamental theorem of algebra: a nonzero degree-d univariate polynomial has at most d roots
modular arithmetic in F_p / finite fields
assumed_background: From Ch 6: arithmetization, the spreadsheet metaphor, and the Schwartz–Zippel *intuition* given as a slogan (‘checking one random cell catches a forged table’), not yet proved
From Ch 1–2: random challenges, bounded error, and soundness as a felt promise (‘almost always’)
Polynomial evaluation over a finite set of values; what the degree of a polynomial means
A finite field F is a set of |F| elements with arithmetic in which a nonzero polynomial cannot have more roots than its degree
learning_objectives: State the (univariate) Schwartz–Zippel lemma: a nonzero degree-d polynomial over F is zero at a uniformly random point with probability at most d/|F|.
Explain why two distinct degree-d polynomials can agree at no more than d of the |F| field points, so a random spot-check catches a difference with probability ≥ 1 - d/|F|.
Derive the bound from the bounded fundamental theorem of algebra (≤ d roots) applied to the difference polynomial p-q, with no extra machinery.
Compute the bound on a concrete field (e.g. F_101) and read off the failure probability (here d/|F| = 1/101).

Connect the lemma to its book role: it licenses probabilistic polynomial-identity testing — reducing a whole-table / whole-polynomial claim to one random evaluation — which underpins arithmetization soundness (R1CS / AIR / PLONKish) and the sum-check and PCS machinery built on top.
Distinguish the unconditional, collision-*bounded* Schwartz–Zippel guarantee from a computational, collision-*resistant* cryptographic hash (a Ch 11/12 distinction not to be conflated).

outgoing_edges: Schwartz-Zippel Lemma --defines--> The Spreadsheet Metaphor (And Where It Works)
Schwartz-Zippel Lemma --references--> The Constraint System Evolution: R1CS, AIR, PLONKish
Schwartz-Zippel Lemma --assumes--> Lemma 2.1: a(X^2)=a(X)^2 iff a is a monomial (polynomial-identity reasoning)
graphify_subgraph.interpretation: The live graph places Schwartz–Zippel in community 7 (arithmetization / constraint-systems), where it *defines* the spreadsheet metaphor's soundness and is *referenced* by R1CS/AIR/PLONKish and the certificate-sealing step. This confirms the chapter framing: the lemma is the probabilistic-checking primitive beneath Layer 4, used to reduce a whole-table claim to one random point. The brief's role objective and the downstream prerequisites (sum-check, PCS) are taken directly from these edges.
